moment_text: Coming from a background of dance, there's a very well-known history there that can be rooted in a lot of mental health issues, especially in the era that I went through the system of professional dance training, just a lot of body image issues. There wasn't a lot of care taken around our mental health states. It was all about the physicality and everything we were training there. So I came through that system and really had to learn for myself. How to operate in this world positively and how to empower myself into the woman I am now. And it's been a very long process of that. And I suppose my inspiration within that is that I hope that I can inspire women to find that sooner or, you know, not have to take that long to figure that out and inspire women to find the, the joy in celebrating their womanhood the way I do as well.
eligibility_rationale: This moment passes the evidence gate (A2) because it contains clear barrier signals (education_gap, training_gap) related to a lack of mental health support within a specific system (professional dance training).
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker reflects on her entire journey, making 'cross_life_stage' the most appropriate tag. The 'system of professional dance training' is categorized as 'education_system'.

moment_text: Absolutely. I started roller, no, sorry, roller skating. I started dancing when I was seven because my next door neighbor was going to dance classes and invited me along and I asked mom and I said, can I go to dance class? My next door neighbor Lauren? And she said, yeah, okay. And since that day I have, I remember that first class so clearly. I remember just being wide-eyed, fascinated by it, and so invested. And I can't even say that. That energy has dropped one day since I've just been, the passion was very quick to grab me and I've been absolutely invested in dance and everything it brings to my life ever since. And it's always been been a very one track mind of I'm gonna, whatever I'm gonna do in life, it's gonna be involving dance. And somehow, and that definitely presented itself in a very, I was a very dedicated dancer, very. Not, not just disciplined, but just very committed. I wanted to be a part of every dance troupe I could be in. I wanted to do every musical, I wanted to do as many dance styles as I could, as much as I possibly could, and definitely was that kid that had way too many extracurricular activities. But I wouldn't be, I, I, I couldn't miss a class. I was sometimes. Arriving at class sick because I was just, I couldn't miss it. It was, there was nothing that was gonna stop me from being there. And the highlights of my year were my end of year dance concerts and any competitions I had in throughout the year. And a, a big part of the, that journey with that dance school, actually, I was very nurtured in that school. It's not a very typical. Dance mom school that you would say, I had a really beautiful school to be nurtured in. It was all about the love of dance and not so much about competitive dance, and I think that was a really key factor into the nurturing, like the, the ability for me to move into a career in dance because I didn't lose my love for it in that process.

moment_text: Big part of that was a focus on the choreographic elements of dance. So most of my competitions when I was young was based around me creating my own pieces and putting them on stage. So I've actually been doing that. Since I was about nine years old. So it's been a very long practice that I've, um, come to this point now, and it's a full circle moment that I just make up these little bedroom, my, my little dances in my bedroom and go out and film somewhere because it's basically what I love to do as a kid.

moment_text: And I, um, moved myself down to Melbourne. I was, I'm from Brisbane and I moved to Melbourne at the age of 17. 'cause I was just one track mind following that dream. And it was, it was an incredible year of learning, but also a very shaping year in a lot of different ways. It was a very different energy to what I grew up with in the sense that I came from a very nurturing environment and this then became very competitive and a lot of image-based criticisms and things like that. And I can definitely account a lot of what I had to unlearn from those formative years, unfortunately. And. At the time, it just wasn't a practice to teach women about proper nutrition and things like that, and, and also to look after their, their mental state when it comes to rejection, because unfortunately, the life of a dancer, you are walking straight into a pathway of constant rejection where we are gonna, even if you are an incredible dancer, you're gonna encounter. A rejection at some point, and learning how to deal with that was something I had to do on the job basically, rather than being prepared for that beforehand.
